titleStmt.title: Letter from Sir Wilfrid Spender to his wife Lady Lillian Spender, 20 August 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Wilfrid Bliss Spender

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: A letter from Sir Wilfrid Bliss Spender (1876-1960) to his wife, Lady Lillian Spender, née Dean, (1880-1968). In this letter, Spender promises only 'a short scrawl', as he plans to drive out to two different corps to gather information on their history. He expresses concern at Lillian's heavy workload and recounts that he himself was up past midnight preparing a 'highly coloured' communiqué for the press - a task not helped by the fact that his various superiors all prefer different styles of writing. Spender also discusses financial matters, making particular reference to the rapidly falling price of rubber.Sir Wilfrid Spender was a member of the British Army until 1913 when controversy surrounding his signature of the Ulster Solemn League and Covenant resulted in his resignation. Spender was called up for military service in 1914 and from 1915 served in the 36th (Ulster) Division. Throughout the war, Spender wrote a collection of letters to his wife Lady Lillian Spender. The letters date mainly from 1915 to 1918, when Sir Wilfrid was serving in France. The letters concern politics, the Irish question, personal life, and Wilfrid's experience of the Great War. Lady Lillian served in the Ulster Volunteer Nursing Corps during the war, and was an organiser of the Ulster Division Comforts Fund.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Aug 20 My pr After my long letter yesterday you will only get a
short paragraph today as I am preparing to go for a long motor
down to visit 2 of the corps in the back areas to collect
history. As I only got no pink pad marks yesterday, I contented myself
very fairly by again following an old trail of the pr's
which I had kept back as reading to rather extra nice
expeditions, and read it after (very) early service 7.30 AM rather
a halo today, but I wondered if you turned up before I left church. I wonder what you are deciding to do, not I hope
getting worried by trying to force yourself to go on with work
or by feeling too tired to make decisions for yourself tho'
ready to do so continually at your own job. Yesterday I did not finish my communiquÃ© till
past midnight though I had had it passed at 9pm & sent
out. Unluckily the C in C likes it at dinner and being
toured by an article in Land & Water which said 'It is
no thanks to the official communiquÃ©s that we have learnt
the important bit of news that POZIERES mill has been
taken', insisted on a highly coloured account going out
at once which Butler Davidson & myself proceeded to
decoct! If only I knew which particular client I had to
paint for it would make it so much easier, but while
the C in C likes the colours of a Turner, Kiggell with whom
I must agree- tho' called dismal by those who don't know him-
likes the more subdued old dutch print style, Butler
likes a nice little etching, and Charters through whom
I first pass my wares has a distinct liking for the glaring
Christmas coloured print. However we all take it in good
part and I on;y wish one's seniors could be spared the thought
& bother of the thing, tho' I shrewdly suspect it is a kind
of hobby more like a chain problem. Let me know when you have quite made up your
mind about the maids. I don't intend to influence you
against your judgement. By the way I enclose Maguire's business letter.
If you like either of them better than my choice of the
(NS Wales!) new loan, you can purchase them it instead with your Â£300 . It
means a little higher rate of interest at a little less
security - the 2 go together of course -. It is jolly good
for you to have this business! You can consult the Pater What I should like to
do is to ensure that our income from safe investments and
pay and exclusions of rubber is sufficient to give us Â£800
a year, so that we had the rubber to play with in getting
our hutch and extras. At present with retired pay it is
about Â£600 we can count on, so that we read another Â£4000
to be invested, of which thanks to the pr we shall have done
Â£1500 when our war certificates mature. At present rubber is
giving us nearly Â£1500 a year but this cannot continue very long
in spite of a pr's care. Rubber profits have indeed half halved
themselves already in the past 6 months! Your bw
WB Spender
